sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaint & incident logging** – no formal escalation path for injuries, facility issues, or service complaints; critical for duty of care & insurance
- **Churn analysis & win-back campaign** – you track retention metrics but no SOP on *how* to identify at-risk members or execute re-engagement sequences
- **Financial reconciliation & month-end close** – no documented process for cash handling, payment disputes, or revenue verification (especially if using multiple payment processors)
- **Staff disciplinary & performance management** – no formal process for coaching quality issues, attendance problems, or capability conversations
- **Health & safety incident response** – no step-by-step for reporting, documenting, or investigating accidents (slip/fall, equipment failure, medical emergency)
- **New coach onboarding (Week 1–4)** – your 17 coach SOPs likely cover *ongoing* coaching; missing the critical first-month integration (systems access, studio tour, shadowing schedule, probation checkpoint)
- **Waitlist & class capacity management** – no documented rules for full classes, cancellations, or member communication when slots fill
- **Supplier & vendor management** – no process for equipment maintenance contracts, cleaning schedules, or emergency repairs

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Marketing SOPs (10/12)** – likely missing: paid ad pause/resume triggers, seasonal campaign planning calendar, and member referral incentive mechanics
- **Email Newsletter & Blog Post SOPs** – exist but probably underdated; check: approval workflows, content calendar integration, frequency cadence, analytics review loops
- **Client onboarding** – verify it covers: induction fitness assessments, goal-setting, class booking rules, and cancellation policy *teaching* (not just enrollment)
- **Coach Daily/Weekly SOPs** – confirm they include: how to handle a member injury

kpi.advice:
# REVENUE — FILL THE BIGGER SPACE & STOP THE CHURN SPIRAL

**Member acquisition is lagging the studio upgrade.** You've 147 members in June (target unclear, but post-relocation you should be growing faster). Revenue is only £20.2k in May, down to £17.7k in June (down 12%), despite adding 25 members since May. This signals pricing power is weak or class utilisation is poor.

- **Raise membership price by 8–12%.** Your AR/M (Average Revenue per Member) has collapsed from £170.43 (Dec) to £120.48 (June) — a 29% drop. Reposition around the bigger, better studio and lock in existing members at £45–50/month (vs. implied £120/month rate today suggests a tiered offer: invert it). Test a £10 introductory month, then £49/month; upsell PT and class packs at sign-up.
- **Kill the 5.74% churn in May and 0% in June is good — but 6.6% in March and 8.33% in April signals post-move member drop-off.** Implement 30-day onboarding calls, studio tour bundles, and buddy sign-ups. Target churn to <2% by August.
- **Add two paid revenue lines by month-end:** (1) Childcare/supervised kids' space during peak AM classes (£8–12/session, 4 sessions/week = £160–240/month incremental), (2) Retail corner (branded water bottles, resistance bands, protein — 20% margin, £150–300/month).

---

# EXPENSES — JUNE COST SPIKE IS THE REAL PROBLEM

**Non-people costs exploded.** May non-people expenses were £9.2k; June jumped to £9.4k despite revenue falling 12%. People costs also crept to £3.7k (up from £3.55k in May). This is where the margin collapsed from 29% (May) to 5% (June).

- **Audit the £9.4k non-people spend immediately.** Likely culprits: utilities/rent for the bigger space, insurance, cleaning, supplies. Negotiate a fixed-cost cap with landlord (if rent is variable); bundle utilities and lock a 12-month rate; renegotiate cleaning contract to variable hours (pay per class, not flat fee). Target: hold non-people costs to £7.5k–£8k even as revenue grows to £24k.
- **Staff costs (£3.7

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is overloaded at 133 sessions.** Redistribute her Personal Training (44 sessions) between Grace and Eilis — JoJo should focus on technical classes (Build, Mobility, Circuits) where her expertise is essential. Aim for JoJo at 100–110 sessions.

• **Grace is under-utilised at 52 sessions despite being founder.** She should take more Build: Lower and Build: Upper classes (her strength) across Wed–Fri to balance the timetable and free JoJo. Realistic target: 70–75 sessions while respecting her Monday-only constraint.

• **Eilis has capacity for growth.** Increase her Personal Training load (currently 11 vs JoJo's 44) and assign more Build: Full Body and Elevate classes on Wed–Fri. She can safely handle 60–65 sessions without overstretch.

• **Body Composition Scans are spread thin.** All three coaches do them; consolidate with JoJo and Grace (20 and 9 respectively) to free Eilis for group classes where she can build confidence and client connection.

• **Tuesday technical classes rely too heavily on JoJo.** Ensure Grace or Eilis cover at least one Mobility or Circuits slot Wed–Fri so members aren't dependent on a single coach and JoJo's schedule breathes.

class_stats.suggestion:
**1. Consolidate low-attendance classes and reallocate slots to "Build" classes.** Engine, Capacity, Barbelle, and Strongevity are consistently underattended (12-52 attendees over 90 days). Cancel or merge these into fewer weekly slots and use the freed-up spots for additional Build: Upper and Build: Lower classes, which have sustained demand.

**2. Shift classes to peak days and promote Monday sessions.** Schedule more Build classes on Wednesday-Friday when attendance is highest. For Monday (361 attendees vs 552 on Wednesday), introduce one Build class or remarket existing slots—the 191-person gap suggests scheduling mismatch rather than lack of interest.

**3. Test a weekend strategy for Saturday (203 attendees).** Saturday is your quietest day. Either run one high-demand Build class as a weekend option to capture members who can't attend weekdays, or reduce Saturday slots and reallocate instructors to strengthen weekday offerings.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Execute: Service Menu Clarity for The Forge

**1. Start with member feedback interviews (not guessing)**
Schedule 15-minute calls with 10-12 current members this week. Ask: "What services are you confused about? What would you pay more for?" This data beats assumptions. Use their exact language in your final menu—it sells better than corporate copy.

**2. Audit your current offerings ruthlessly**
List every service you actually deliver (classes, PT sessions, nutrition coaching, assessments, etc.). Note which ones: generate revenue, take coach time, create confusion. Kill or bundle the bottom 20%. This clarity saves your coaches from explaining 15 options per member.

**3. Build two separate menus with different jobs**
- **Coach menu**: Internal pricing sheet, time-per-service, profit margins, package combos (so they can upsell naturally in conversations)
- **Member menu**: Visual, benefits-focused (not feature-heavy), clear price anchoring—e.g., "£30/class OR £60/month unlimited" so price becomes obvious, not a negotiation point

**4. Price using your numbers, not competitors**
Calculate: coach hourly cost + overhead. Set prices that hit your target margin *per service*. Don't undercut Belfast competitors—position on *women's space + community* instead. Test 1:1 PT at your target price on 3 new members. Adjust if needed.

**5. Embed it into onboarding today**
New member joins? Hand them the menu, walk them through it in their first week. Coaches reference it in every conversation. This prevents "What else do you offer?" from becoming a revenue leak.

What's your biggest service

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement - The Forge

**1. Audit your current drop-off points first (30 mins)**
Pull analytics from your landing pages—where are visitors exiting? Is it after the headline? After pricing? After seeing "women-only"? Fix the biggest leak first, not everything at once. This tells you which clarity gap costs you most conversions.

**2. Steal your FAQ from real prospect conversations**
Don't guess what questions matter. Text/call your last 10 enquiries who *didn't* join and ask: "What stopped you deciding?" Record exact phrases. These become your landing page copy. Common ones for women's gyms: "Will I be judged on fitness level?" "Is it actually all-female or just women's classes?" "What if I'm nervous about lifting?"

**3. Lead with ONE clear differentiator, not a feature list**
Women-only is your hook—but *why* does it matter to your ideal member? Is it comfort? Intensity without distraction? Community? Pick one, lead with it in a 10-word headline, then support it with social proof (member testimonials about feeling empowered, not intimidated).

**4. Add a "confidence section" before the CTA**
Before asking for a sign-up, address the biggest objection women face: "What if I'm a beginner?" Show one before/after story or quote like *"I couldn't do a press-up. Now I can."* This kills the friction that stops click-to-call.

**5. Test one change weekly, measure signup rate**
Change the headline this week. Track enquiries. Change the CTA button color next week. This isn't guesswork—

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Feedback System — Execution Plan for The Forge

• **Start ridiculously small: 3-question post-class form.** Not a survey. Ask: (1) "What worked today?" (2) "One thing to improve?" (3) "Would you recommend us?" Print on receipt-sized cards, pen on clipboard by exit. Takes 60 seconds. More responses = more data than a sprawling form no one fills.

• **Make it a ritual, not a chore.** Assign ONE staff member per shift to hand it out during the cooldown stretch—make it part of class wrap-up. Tie it to something: "We read these on Tuesday mornings and act on them." Creates accountability loop members see.

• **Route responses into a shared Google Sheet, not a folder.** You need to spot patterns fast. Monthly: sort by theme (class timing, equipment, instructor style, facility), flag the top 3, pick ONE to fix visibly. Example: "You said we needed better ventilation—we upgraded the fans this month."

• **Close the loop publicly.** Post a monthly 1-minute video or newsletter: "3 things you told us + what we changed." This turns feedback into proof you listen. Members become evangelists because they *see their input matters*. Word-of-mouth grows.

• **Bonus: Add a 1-question Typeform link in WhatsApp/email monthly.** Captures members who can't/won't write by hand. Same 3 questions. Merge data weekly.

What's your biggest hesitation on rolling this out?

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOPs for The Forge — Execution Strategy

**1. Start with revenue impact ranking, not alphabetical order**
Do Fuel Forward first (highest recurring revenue + easiest to standardize), then Personal Training (your premium service that needs consistency). Save Well75/Strongevity for phase two. You'll build momentum and have proof-of-concept to show staff before tackling more complex programs.

**2. Use a single template that mirrors your actual member journey**
Create one master template: Sign-up process → First session/interaction → Ongoing member touchpoints → Retention/upsell moments → Exit/pause. Fill this in for each service. Avoids creating five separate documents that contradict each other.

**3. Record a 5-10 min video walkthrough for EACH service**
Write the SOP, then film yourself or a top staff member executing it. Post on your internal system (Asana, Notion, Google Drive — pick one). Text-only SOPs get ignored; video SOPs get watched during downtime. This is non-negotiable for training new PTs.

**4. Embed the business rule, not just the task**
Don't just write "Send membership welcome email." Write: "Send welcome email within 24 hours because 67% of cancellations happen in week 1 — we need immediate engagement." Staff follow instructions they understand.

**5. Assign one person as "SOP owner" per service for 90 days**
They own updating it based on what breaks. SOPs die when nobody owns them. Pay a small bonus for quality documentation (£50-100/service). You'll get living documents, not shelf-ware.

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar for The Forge

**The Real Execution:**

• **Start with 8 weeks of templates, not a blank calendar.** Build 4-5 repeating content "slots" (e.g., Monday = member spotlight, Wednesday = quick workout tip, Friday = class schedule highlight, Saturday = community win). Pre-design these in Canva once, then swap in different people/details weekly. This kills the "what do I post?" paralysis that stops most gym owners.

• **Batch-record content in one 90-minute session monthly.** Get 4-5 members on camera doing testimonials or demonstrating moves. Film 10-15 30-second clips raw. This eliminates the daily pressure and gives you a stockpile. Upload 2-3 per week using a scheduling tool (Buffer, Later).

• **Assign this to one person (or automate it immediately).** If Grace does this herself, it dies in week 3 when she's running the gym. Give a staff member or hire a £15/hour part-time person to manage uploads using your templates. Non-negotiable.

• **Track what actually drives inquiries.** Before you build the calendar, spend 2 weeks posting organic stuff and asking new members "how did you find us?" Every post should link back to conversions, not vanity metrics. Then build your calendar around what works for *your* audience.

• **Leverage existing content.** Screenshots of member transformations, class energy clips, behind-the-scenes gym setup—film what's already happening. Don't create fake content. Authenticity converts better for women's fitness spaces.

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Scheduling Videography Sessions with Jordan — Here's What Works:

• **Lock it in NOW as a standing quarterly appointment.** Don't wait for "the right time." Email Jordan today with 3 specific dates 8 weeks apart (e.g., Feb 15, May 10, Aug 15) and ask him to block them. This removes the friction of rescheduling later and ensures content pipeline consistency.

• **Tie each session to a business milestone or seasonal angle.** Don't just film for filming's sake. Schedule around New Year transformations, summer body season, or when you're launching a new class. Tell Jordan the *theme* upfront so he comes with a shot list—this makes the footage actually usable.

• **Build a simple content calendar backwards from these dates.** If you're filming Feb 15, you need edited content ready by Feb 25 for social rollout. This forces you to batch-edit between shoots and actually *use* what you're paying for—most gyms waste 60% of video content.

• **Set a 90-minute session scope to keep costs down.** Agree with Jordan: 90 mins gets you 4-5 polished reels/stories worth of material. Any longer and you're paying for diminishing returns; any shorter and you're not capturing enough variety.

• **Add a quick debrief clause.** After each shoot, spend 15 mins with Jordan identifying what worked (members loved X exercise, Y angle got engagement). Iterate the next shoot based on this—your content gets better *and* more strategic each time.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Automating 6-Week Trial Enquiries — The Forge

**Cut the manual friction:**
- Set up a 3-email sequence in your booking/CRM system (Calendly, Mindbody, or even Mailchimp) that triggers the *moment* someone submits a trial enquiry: Day 1 (welcome + scheduling link), Day 14 (check-in: "How's week 2?"), Day 35 (upsell: membership options). This removes the "chase them" work entirely.

**Make the second touchpoint actually valuable:**
- That Day 14 email isn't just "how's it going?"—include a 30-second video of *you* or a trainer showing a common form fix or member win story. Women-only gyms succeed on community; automate the *connection*, not just the admin.

**Segment the drop-offs:**
- Build a simple rule: if someone doesn't show up for sessions 1-3, trigger a different (softer) sequence asking "What's blocking you?" vs. those who *are* attending but haven't converted. You're automating *response*, not assuming.

**Track the actual conversion metric daily:**
- Automate a weekly Slack/email summary showing: trials started → attended 3+ sessions → converted to member. You'll spot friction (bad onboarding day? pricing hesitation?) *while you can still fix it*, not in hindsight.

**One setup rule:** keep it to 3-4 touchpoints max. Automated doesn't mean spammy. Women especially respond to *quality* over volume.

What system is The Forge currently using for trial bookings?

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Executing Role-Based Action Checklists + KPIs for The Forge

**1. Start with revenue/retention impact, not job descriptions**
Don't assign tasks—assign outcomes. For each person, work backwards from your #1 growth lever (new member acquisition? retention? class utilization?). Ask: "What does JoJo own that directly moves revenue?" That becomes her KPI first, then her checklist reverse-engineers backwards. Example: If JoJo drives member onboarding, her KPI is "Day-7 retention rate %" and her weekly checklist includes the 3-4 touchpoints she controls to hit it.

**2. Make KPIs ridiculously specific and weekly-trackable**
Vague = ignored. Instead of "improve member experience," JoJo's KPI is "conduct 6 intro calls on Wed/Thu, achieve 80% conversion to trial class." Sarah's might be "retain 92% of members paying this week, conduct 2 churn rescue calls." Put the actual number, the day it's measured, what triggers action if it's missed.

**3. Each person owns 2-3 KPIs max, not 10**
Spread accountability across the three. Let them specialize. Eilis might own referral conversion + retention metrics, JoJo owns acquisition pipeline + onboarding, Sarah owns revenue collection + class fill rates. Write it down as: "Eilis KPI: X referrals converted to paid members per week."

**4. Use a dead-simple one-page format per person**
Single A4 page: role name, 2-3 weekly KPIs with targets, 5-

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Team Vision Session Execution for Grace

• **Structure it as a 90-minute "contract signing" not a lecture** — Open with 15 mins of you explaining WHY the SSO matters to YOU personally (what winning looks like for The Forge), then spend 60 mins having each team member articulate THEIR role in the 90-day plan back to you. Make them own it, not receive it.

• **Make lanes visible on a wall, not a slide** — Build a physical/digital board showing the 90-day priorities with names attached. Have team members add their top 3 actions under their lane *during* the session. They walk out with specific ownership, not vague assignments.

• **Kill the "any questions?" trap** — Instead ask: "Where will you need help from someone else?" and "What's one thing that might block you?" This surfaces real friction early instead of nodding heads that nod back into old habits.

• **Schedule 1-on-1s for the next week, not after the session** — The session plants seeds. Individual check-ins cement them. Each person needs 20 mins with you to translate their lane into actual weekly commitments so it sticks past Friday.

• **Reinforce with a 1-page visual they keep at their desk** — Your SSO, their lane, their 3 key actions. Low-friction reminder beats email chain follow-ups.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalancing for The Forge

• **Map actual hours, not job titles** — Have each team member log activities for 5 working days (time-tracking app or even a shared spreadsheet). You'll spot the real bottleneck fast—it's rarely where you think. Sales staff drowning in admin? Instructors doing double-duty with member onboarding? Numbers don't lie.

• **Identify the 3-person crisis point** — In a women's gym, it's usually: (1) someone covering reception + sales calls, (2) instructor doing programming + cleaning, (3) you handling billing + retention. Fix the ONE role that's breaking first—that's your 30-day win.

• **Trade tasks by profit impact, not politeness** — Don't just "split things evenly." Move low-value work OFF your highest-earner (the instructor pulling in class fees). Shift high-touch member-facing tasks TO your sales-focused person. Match capacity to revenue generation.

• **Quick-win cross-training** — Identify ONE admin task (member check-ins, class scheduling, payment follow-ups) that two people can learn in a week. Spreads risk AND covers holidays/sick days without hiring.

• **Set a 2-week checkpoint** — Week 2, not week 30. Ask each person: "Is this sustainable?" If the answer's no, you've identified what needs outsourcing, automating, or restructuring—not just reshuffling the same overload.

What's your biggest suspected bottleneck right now?

# Cutting 10% at The Forge — Here's What Actually Works

• **Audit your top 3 cost buckets first (60 seconds).** For a gym: rent, staff payroll, and equipment maintenance eat ~70% of budget. Don't waste time nickel-and-diming supplies. Ask your landlord about lease renegotiation (cite market comps for Belfast gym space), and review if you're overstaffed during off-peak hours — you might cut 1-2 shifts weekly without impacting member experience.

• **Renegotiate vendor contracts NOW, not in 6 months.** Call your equipment supplier, cleaning service, and utilities provider with this pitch: "I've got competing quotes at X% lower — can you match or beat it?" Most will. Utilities alone might drop 5-8% with a 3-year lock-in. Do this in the next 2 weeks while you have leverage.

• **Cut one premium service that doesn't drive retention.** Women's gyms often over-index on perks (premium shampoo, fancy towel warmers, smoothie bar). Audit last quarter's actual usage — if sauna maintenance is costing £200/month but members skip it, pull it. Keep what drives your NPS and revenue; kill the rest.

• **Eliminate waste in your schedule/workflow.** If staff spend 30 mins daily on manual admin, a £30/month booking app integration saves £2-3k/year. That's half your 10% target with zero member impact.

**Target: Hit 7-8% in week one (vendor + staffing), find the last 2-3% through one service cut.**

# Updating Monthly Finance KPIs — Here's How Grace Should Actually Do It

• **Pull the data in one sitting, same day each month** — Designate the 2nd business day after month-end as "KPI day." Grab bank statements, Stripe/payment processor reports, and membership software in one go. This prevents data scattered across weeks and reduces errors.

• **Focus on 5 metrics only, not a spreadsheet graveyard** — Track: (1) Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRIs × avg membership price), (2) Churn rate (cancellations ÷ total members), (3) Member acquisition cost (marketing spend ÷ new sign-ups), (4) Gross margin (revenue minus direct costs), (5) Cash position. Anything else dilutes focus.

• **Build a one-page visual dashboard, not a report** — Use Google Sheets with simple charts showing trend lines month-over-month. Grace should see at a glance if churn spiked or acquisition cost jumped. Update it in 20 minutes, not 2 hours analyzing.

• **Use KPI review to trigger action, not just logging** — When a metric moves, it must trigger a specific decision within 48 hours (e.g., if churn >5%, email inactive members; if acquisition cost rises, pause ad channels). Otherwise, updating KPIs becomes busywork.

• **Automate what you can, own what you can't** — Sync membership software directly to the sheet if possible. Grace personally reviews churn (needs her judgment on retention strategy), but payment data should flow automatically.

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Don't build a 3-year model yet.** Start with 12 months of actual numbers (last year's revenue by month, member churn patterns, class utilization rates). This anchors your forecasts in reality. Grace, grab your POS data and membership software exports *this week* — this is your baseline.

• **Model three scenarios, not one.** Build worst-case (10% member drop), base-case (current trajectory), and upside (aggressive marketing adds 15% new members). Run each through your P&L. This teaches you which levers actually move your bottom line and where to stress-test assumptions.

• **Forecast backwards from cash, not profit.** Most gyms fail on cash flow, not profitability. Map when membership fees hit your account, when supplier invoices are due, and payroll dates. If you're running a promotion in January, you need to know if you have cash to cover February wages before members pay.

• **Assign ONE number owner monthly.** Grace or a trusted manager reviews actuals vs. forecast the first Monday of each month (takes 30 mins). Note the variance — if you forecasted 120 active members and have 105, *diagnose why* before month-end. This isn't accounting; it's your early warning system.

• **Link forecasts directly to one growth metric.** Pick either new member acquisition cost or member lifetime value, calculate it monthly, and watch it like a hawk. This keeps financial forecasting connected to actual growth decisions, not spreadsheet theater.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge — Action Plan

**Start with 4 weeks of data capture (not perfect data):**
- Assign one person 30 mins/week to log: class attendance (by type), PT session bookings, add-on sales (smoothies, retail, etc.). Use a simple Google Sheet with columns: Date | Service | Revenue | Direct Costs (instructor pay, products). Don't wait for "complete" data—move with what you have.

**Build a quick-and-dirty P&L per service:**
- Create three sections: Classes (split by class type if volume varies), PT, Add-ons. For each, calculate: Total Revenue ÷ Total Hours/Sessions = Revenue per Unit. Subtract instructor costs, product costs, allocated facility overhead (rent ÷ number of active services). This reveals your profit-per-service immediately—no accounting software needed.

**Identify your margin outliers within 2 weeks:**
- Which class type is packed but unprofitable (high attendance, low margin)? Which add-on is a quiet profit engine? Use this to kill or reprice low-margin services fast—don't carry them hoping volume improves.

**Act on findings by week 3:**
- If PT margins exceed classes, shift marketing budget and staff incentives toward PT growth. If a class type crushes it, add a second session. If smoothies have 70% margins, move them to the busiest class exit point and train staff to upsell.

**One caveat:** Don't let "I need more data" paralyze you. A 70% accurate P&L you act on beats a 100% accurate one gathering dust.

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Execution Plan: Automation Audit for The Forge

• **Pull your full automation stack TODAY** — export workflows from Zapier/Make/whatever you're using, plus any email sequences in your CRM. List every trigger-action pair. You'll likely spot 3-5 duplicates within 30 mins (common: multiple "new enquiry" triggers firing the same welcome email).

• **Map the actual journey, not the intended one** — have someone pose as a cold enquiry right now. Where does her email land? Does she get the trial booking link? When? Does she get follow-ups if she doesn't book? Document what *actually* happens vs. what should happen. This catches broken integrations (e.g., Zapier → CRM sync failing silently).

• **Kill the duplicates ruthlessly** — if two automations do similar things, delete one. But before you do: check which one has more detailed data/conditions. Keep the stronger version. Delete the zombie automations (those created 6+ months ago, probably forgotten).

• **Test the critical path in isolation** — turn off everything except: enquiry form → welcome email → trial booking email → follow-up sequence. Does a woman go from form to booked trial in under 2 hours? If not, find the gap (delayed email, missing link, wrong Zapier connection).

• **Set a quarterly audit calendar entry** — 30 mins every 13 weeks. Prevents this mess rebuilding. Check: bounce rates, unsubscribe spikes, and enquiries that never convert to trials.

**Owner: Grace. Timeline: 2 hours today, full fix by Friday.**

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — Concrete Execution

**Pick these 3 tasks first (highest ROI for a gym):**
1. **New member onboarding emails** — automate the welcome sequence (day 1, day 3, week 1, month 1). Use Zapier + ChatGPT or native automations in your booking software to trigger based on signup date
2. **Class booking reminders & no-show follow-ups** — set automated SMS/email 24hrs before class, then auto-message no-shows offering a reschedule link
3. **Instagram caption + hashtag generation** — batch create weekly captions for gym wins, member spotlights, class schedules using ChatGPT; schedule via Buffer

**How Grace executes THIS week:**
- **Audit your current flow**: Track how many hours these tasks eat per week (likely 4-6 hours total)
- **Pick your automation platform**: Zapier (easiest, connects everything), Make (more powerful), or native tools in your booking software (fastest setup)
- **Template first, automate second**: Write 3-4 email templates + 5 SMS templates; test them live for 1 week before full automation
- **Measure the win**: Track time saved × hourly rate = your ROI per month (you'll likely free up 3-4 hours/week worth £30-50/week to reinvest in growth)

**Real next step**: Spend 90 minutes mapping your current onboarding process end-to-end today. That's your automation blueprint.

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity for The Forge Website — Execution Plan

• **Audit first, then act** — Run a free security scan (use Sucuri or WordPress security plugins if you're on WP) to identify actual vulnerabilities before spending money. Many gyms are targets for payment/membership data theft. Get a baseline, then prioritize fixes by risk level.

• **Lock down payments immediately** — If you're processing card payments on-site, migrate to a PCI-compliant third-party processor (Stripe, Square). Don't store card data yourself. This is your biggest liability and easiest quick win for member trust.

• **Get an SSL certificate live today** — Non-negotiable. Your domain needs the green padlock. Most hosts offer free Let's Encrypt; if not, it's £10/year. Do this before you tell anyone to book or pay online.

• **Implement member login protection now** — Add two-factor authentication (2FA) for accounts. Use something simple members won't abandon (SMS OTP, Google Authenticator). This prevents account takeovers and membership sharing.

• **One person owns this end-to-end** — Designate yourself or a trusted team member as "cybersecurity lead." Assign them 4-6 hours this week to execute the above, then monthly check-ins. No process dies from unclear ownership.

**Real talk:** Start with the payment/data security items. That's where your legal and reputational risk lives.

marketing.advice:
# The Forge – June Ad Performance Analysis

## Insights

**Lead quality is collapsing.** Cost-per-lead has tripled from £6 (April) to £20 (June), yet close rate has halved from 29% (April: 20/69) to 48% (June). You're paying 3.3x more to get leads that convert at half the rate. This isn't a volume problem—it's a targeting or creative decay problem.

**Your audience is exhausted or your message has gone stale.** April's 69 leads at £6 suggests you were hitting warm, intent-rich women. June's 21 leads at £20 suggests you're either retargeting dead accounts, bidding into irrelevant searches, or your creative stopped resonating. The spend drop (£417 vs £435/£522) also hints you've throttled budget—possibly because you noticed deterioration.

**Close rate staying at 48% (vs 29-61% prior months) masks the real problem.** The 10 sales in June are your lowest absolute number. Even if conversion improved, you're generating half the pipeline. Profit collapsed from £1850 (May, rough calc: 23 × ~£80 margin) to £1538 because lead volume dried up, not because sales quality improved.

## Actions

**Audit and segment your audience immediately.** Split your targeting into: (1) Lookalikes of April converters only (those 20 sales), (2) Custom audiences of past trial members/enquiries, (3) Broad interest targeting (fitness, wellness, women 18–55, Belfast area). Run each at 10% of budget this week. April's £6/lead likely came from one of these segments performing—find it.

**Pause underperforming creative and rotate in fresh angles.** You've likely hit frequency cap or creative fatigue. Test: (1) before/after testimonials from recent members (48% close rate means you have happy customers—use them), (2) "6-week challenge" framing vs generic "trial" (urgency/specificity), (3) pain-point messaging (busy schedules, intimidation by co-ed gyms) instead of benefit-
